subject: mathematics
ncdc_competency: The learner determines the resultant force and analyses the effect of forces on bodies by applying Newton's laws to solve problems in real world phenomena.

lessons.title: Forces, Vectors, and Resultants — Components and Composition

content.body: Dynamics is the branch of mechanics that studies how forces cause motion and maintain equilibrium. Every physical object in the world is subject to forces — gravity pulling it downward, friction resisting its motion, tensions in ropes supporting it, normal reactions from surfaces. Understanding how these forces combine and what motion they produce is fundamental to engineering, architecture, sport science, and everyday life. In Uganda, dynamics governs the design of road vehicles on hilly terrain, the stability of buildings in the Rwenzori foothills, and the forces on boats crossing Lake Victoria. This lesson establishes the foundation: forces as vectors and the methods for finding their resultant.
Forces as Vectors

items: A force is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude (size, measured in Newtons, N) and direction.
Forces are represented by arrows: the length indicates magnitude, the direction of the arrow shows direction.

Common forces: Weight (W = mg, downward), Normal reaction (N, perpendicular to surface), Tension (T, along a string/rope), Friction (F, opposing motion along surface), Applied force (push or pull in a specified direction).
Newton's First Law: a body remains at rest or moves with constant velocity unless acted upon by a resultant (net) force.
Newton's Second Law: F = ma. Resultant force = mass × acceleration.
Newton's Third Law: for every action force, there is an equal and opposite reaction force.
Weight vs mass: mass m (kg) is the amount of matter. Weight W = mg (N) is the gravitational force. On Earth, g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (or g = 10 m/s² for approximate calculations).

content.body: Resolving Forces into Components

Any force can be resolved (split) into two perpendicular components — usually horizontal and vertical. This is the reverse of finding a resultant. Resolving is essential for analysing forces that act at angles to the axes.

For a force F acting at angle θ to the horizontal:

Horizontal component: Fₓ = F cos θ
Vertical component:   F_y = F sin θ

For a force F acting at angle θ to the vertical:
Vertical component:   F_y = F cos θ
Horizontal component: Fₓ = F sin θ

Memory tip: the component in the direction OF the angle uses cosine;
the component PERPENDICULAR to the angle direction uses sine.

Example: A force of 50 N acts at 30° above the horizontal.
Horizontal component = 50 cos 30° = 50 × (√3/2) = 25√3 ≈ 43.3 N
Vertical component = 50 sin 30° = 50 × (1/2) = 25 N
Finding the Resultant of Two or More Forces

The resultant of a system of forces is the single force that has the same effect as all the forces acting together. There are two main methods: the triangle/parallelogram law (geometrical) and the component method (algebraic).

content.heading: Method 1 — Triangle of Forces (geometrical):
items: For two forces P and Q at angle θ between them: R² = P² + Q² + 2PQ cos θ (cosine rule).
Direction of R: tan α = (Q sin θ)/(P + Q cos θ) where α is the angle R makes with P.
Special cases: Forces at 90° → R = √(P²+Q²). Forces in the same direction → R = P+Q. Forces in opposite directions → R = |P−Q|.
Parallelogram law: draw both forces from the same point; the resultant is the diagonal of the parallelogram.

content.heading: Method 2 — Component method (algebraic, for multiple forces):
items: Step 1: Resolve each force into horizontal (x) and vertical (y) components.
Step 2: Sum all horizontal components: ΣFₓ = F₁cosθ₁ + F₂cosθ₂ + ...
Step 3: Sum all vertical components: ΣF_y = F₁sinθ₁ + F₂sinθ₂ + ...
Step 4: Magnitude of resultant: R = √((ΣFₓ)² + (ΣF_y)²).
Step 5: Direction: tan θ = ΣF_y / ΣFₓ (angle to horizontal).
This method works for any number of forces and is the most systematic approach.

content.title: Example 1: Find the resultant of three forces
content.body:
Three forces act at a point:
F₁ = 10 N at 0° (horizontal, east)
F₂ = 8 N at 90° (vertical, north)
F₃ = 6 N at 150° (north-west direction)

Resolve each:
F₁: x = 10cos0° = 10, y = 10sin0° = 0
F₂: x = 8cos90° = 0, y = 8sin90° = 8
F₃: x = 6cos150° = 6(−√3/2) = −3√3 ≈ −5.196, y = 6sin150° = 6(1/2) = 3

ΣFₓ = 10 + 0 + (−5.196) = 4.804 N
ΣF_y = 0 + 8 + 3 = 11 N

R = √(4.804² + 11²) = √(23.08 + 121) = √144.08 ≈ 12.00 N
tan θ = 11/4.804 → θ = arctan(2.290) ≈ 66.4° above horizontal

Answer: Resultant ≈ 12.0 N at 66.4° above the horizontal (north-east direction).

content.title: Example 2: Resultant of forces in a polygon (force polygon)
content.body:
Four forces act at a point: 5N East, 12N North, 8N West, 3N South.

ΣFₓ = 5 + 0 + (−8) + 0 = −3 N (net westward)
ΣF_y = 0 + 12 + 0 + (−3) = 9 N (net northward)

R = √(9 + 81) = √90 = 3√10 ≈ 9.49 N
tan θ = 9/(−3) → angle in second quadrant.
|θ| = arctan(3) ≈ 71.6° from west toward north.
Bearing from north: 180° + 90° − 71.6° = 198.4°... 
Actually: direction is 3 west, 9 north → bearing = 360° − arctan(3/9) = arctan(1/3) west of north ≈ 18.4° west of north → bearing ≈ 341.6°.

Answer: R ≈ 9.49 N on bearing approximately 342°.

Engineering application: When designing a bridge pier in Uganda (such as the Isimba Hydroelectric Dam bridge), engineers must calculate the resultant of all forces acting on each structural element — self-weight, water pressure, traffic loads, and wind loads. Each force is resolved into components, and the resultant determines the required strength of the pier. Getting the resultant wrong could lead to catastrophic failure.

questions.question: A force of 20 N acts at 60° to the horizontal. Find its horizontal and vertical components.
Two forces of 3 N and 4 N act at right angles. Find the magnitude of the resultant.
questions.explanation: For perpendicular forces: R = √(3² + 4²) = √(9+16) = √25 = 5 N. This is a 3-4-5 right triangle.

questions.question: Three forces act on a particle: 6 N East, 8 N North, 3 N West. Find the resultant magnitude.
A force F has horizontal component 12 N and vertical component 5 N. Find F and the angle it makes with the horizontal.
Two equal forces of magnitude P act at angle 2α between them. The resultant has magnitude:
questions.explanation: Using the parallelogram law: R² = P²+P²+2P²cos(2α) = 2P²(1+cos2α) = 2P²(2cos²α) = 4P²cos²α. R = 2P cos α.

questions.question: Forces of 5 N, 12 N, and 13 N act along three sides of a right-angled triangle. If the 5 N and 12 N forces are perpendicular, their resultant equals:
options: √(169) N in same direction as 13 N
questions.explanation: R = √(5²+12²) = √(25+144) = √169 = 13 N. The resultant of the 5 N and 12 N perpendicular forces is exactly 13 N — matching the third force in magnitude.

questions.question: A 100 N force acts at 45° to the horizontal. Resolve it into components. What is each component?
questions.explanation: At 45°: Fₓ = 100cos45° = 100/√2 = 50√2 ≈ 70.7 N. F_y = 100sin45° = 50√2 ≈ 70.7 N. Both components are equal and equal to 50√2 N. Note: 100/√2 = 50√2 — both expressions A and D are equivalent.

questions.question: If the resultant of two forces P and Q is zero, what must be true about P and Q?
options: P and Q are equal in magnitude and act in the same direction
P and Q are equal in magnitude and act in exactly opposite directions
questions.answer: P and Q are equal in magnitude and act in exactly opposite directions
questions.explanation: For the resultant to be zero (equilibrium), the forces must cancel each other completely. This requires equal magnitudes and opposite directions (acting along the same line but in opposite senses).

lessons.title: Equilibrium of Forces — Lami's Theorem and Conditions

content.body: When a body is in equilibrium, the resultant of all forces acting on it is zero — the body neither accelerates nor rotates. Equilibrium is one of the most important concepts in mechanics. Every structure that remains standing — every bridge, building, dam, and scaffold — is in equilibrium. Engineers design structures to maintain equilibrium under all expected loads. This lesson covers the conditions for equilibrium, triangle of forces, and Lami's theorem — a particularly elegant result for three concurrent forces in equilibrium.
Conditions for Equilibrium

items: For a particle (point mass) to be in equilibrium: the resultant force must be zero.
This means: ΣFₓ = 0 (sum of all horizontal components = 0) AND ΣF_y = 0 (sum of all vertical components = 0).
For an extended body to be in equilibrium: additionally, the sum of moments about any point must be zero (no rotation). This is covered in more advanced mechanics topics.
Triangle of forces: three concurrent forces in equilibrium can be represented as the three sides of a closed triangle taken in order.
The converse is also true: if three forces can be represented as a closed triangle, they are in equilibrium.

content.body: Lami's Theorem

Lami's theorem provides an elegant formula for three concurrent coplanar forces in equilibrium. When three forces P, Q, and R act at a point and are in equilibrium, there is a simple relationship between each force and the angle opposite to it.

Lami's Theorem:
If three concurrent coplanar forces P, Q, and R are in equilibrium, and α, β, γ are the angles between the forces (where α is the angle between Q and R, β is between P and R, and γ is between P and Q), then:

P/sin α = Q/sin β = R/sin γ

Note: α, β, and γ are the angles BETWEEN the forces, not the angles they make with axes. They satisfy: α + β + γ = 360°.

Equivalently, using the angles each force makes with the other forces:
P/sin(angle opposite P) = Q/sin(angle opposite Q) = R/sin(angle opposite R)

This is essentially the sine rule applied to the triangle of forces.

content.title: Example 1: Using Lami's theorem
content.body:
Three forces P, Q = 8 N, and R = 6 N act at a point in equilibrium. The angle between Q and R is 90°, and the angle between P and Q is 150°.

Angle between P and R = 360° − 90° − 150° = 120°.

By Lami's theorem:
P/sin(90°) = Q/sin(120°) = R/sin(150°)

From Q: Q/sin(120°) = 8/(√3/2) = 16/√3

Verify R: R/sin(150°) = 6/(1/2) = 12. But 16/√3 ≈ 9.24 ≠ 12.

Hmm — let's set up correctly using actual angles. Lami's theorem: each force divided by the sine of the angle BETWEEN the OTHER TWO forces.

P/sin(90°) = 8/sin(120°) = 6/sin(150°)?
Check: 8/sin120° = 8/(√3/2) = 16/√3 ≈ 9.24. 6/sin150° = 6/0.5 = 12. Not equal — so these three forces with these angles are NOT in equilibrium. Let me use a proper example.

Three forces in equilibrium: P, Q = 10 N, R = 8 N. Angle between Q and R = 120°, between P and R = 130°, between P and Q = 110°.
P/sin120° = 10/sin130° = 8/sin110°.
Check: 10/sin130° = 10/0.766 ≈ 13.05. 8/sin110° = 8/0.940 ≈ 8.51. Not equal either.

For a clean example: P, Q, R in equilibrium with angles 120° between each pair.
P/sin120° = Q/sin120° = R/sin120° → P = Q = R.
Three equal forces, each separated by 120°, in equilibrium.

content.title: Example 2: Three-force equilibrium using components
content.body:
A particle is held in equilibrium by three forces:
F₁ = 40 N acting horizontally to the right
F₂ = 30 N acting vertically upward
F₃ = unknown force

For equilibrium: ΣFₓ = 0 and ΣF_y = 0.

Let F₃ act at angle θ below the horizontal (to the left and downward):
F₃ₓ = −F₃cosθ and F₃_y = −F₃sinθ

ΣFₓ = 40 − F₃cosθ = 0 → F₃cosθ = 40 ...(1)
ΣF_y = 30 − F₃sinθ = 0 → F₃sinθ = 30 ...(2)

From (1) and (2): F₃ = √(40²+30²) = √(1600+900) = √2500 = 50 N
tanθ = 30/40 = 3/4 → θ = 36.87° below horizontal.

F₃ = 50 N acting at 36.87° below the horizontal, directed to the left and downward.

content.title: Example 3: Object on a smooth inclined plane
content.body:
A box of mass 5 kg rests on a smooth inclined plane making angle 30° with the horizontal, held by a string parallel to the plane. Find the tension T and normal reaction N. (g = 10 m/s²)

Forces acting:
Weight W = 5 × 10 = 50 N (downward)
Normal reaction N (perpendicular to plane, outward)
Tension T (up the plane, along the plane)

Resolve parallel to plane (taking up-plane as positive):
T − W sin30° = 0 (no acceleration along plane)
T = 50 × 0.5 = 25 N

Resolve perpendicular to plane:
N − W cos30° = 0 (no acceleration perpendicular to plane)
N = 50 × (√3/2) = 25√3 ≈ 43.3 N

Answer: T = 25 N, N = 25√3 N ≈ 43.3 N.

Structural application: When a construction crane lifts a steel beam at a building site in Kampala, the steel cable makes an angle with the vertical. The tension in the cable equals the component of the beam's weight along the cable direction, and the crane boom carries the perpendicular component. Engineers use equilibrium equations (ΣF = 0) to determine the required cable strength and boom compression to prevent collapse.

questions.question: For a particle to be in equilibrium under the action of three forces, which condition must hold?
options: The three forces must all be equal in magnitude
The sum of horizontal components = 0 AND sum of vertical components = 0
At least one force must be vertical
The forces must all act in the same direction
questions.answer: The sum of horizontal components = 0 AND sum of vertical components = 0
questions.explanation: Equilibrium requires the resultant force to be zero in all directions. This means ΣFₓ = 0 (horizontal equilibrium) AND ΣF_y = 0 (vertical equilibrium) must both be satisfied simultaneously.

questions.question: A mass of 3 kg hangs from two strings making angles 30° and 60° with the horizontal. Find the tension in the string making 30° with the horizontal. (g = 10 m/s²)
A 10 kg box rests on a smooth plane inclined at 40° to the horizontal, held by a horizontal force F. Find F. (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: Forces: W=100N down, N perpendicular to plane, F horizontal. Resolve along plane: Fcos40° = Wsin40° → F = 100sin40°/cos40° = 100tan40° ≈ 83.9 N.

questions.question: Three forces 5 N, 12 N, and 13 N act at a point. For these to form a triangle of forces (in equilibrium), what must be true?
options: They must form a closed triangle when drawn in sequence
The largest force must be horizontal
They must all act in the same plane
questions.answer: They must form a closed triangle when drawn in sequence
questions.explanation: The triangle of forces theorem states: three forces are in equilibrium if and only if they can be represented as the three sides of a closed triangle, taken in order. Check: 5²+12²=25+144=169=13² ✓ — these form a right triangle, so they can be in equilibrium.

questions.question: A sign of weight 80 N is suspended by two wires making equal angles of 45° with the vertical. Find the tension in each wire.
Using Lami's theorem, if three forces P, Q, R are in equilibrium with equal angles of 120° between each pair, what is the relationship between P, Q, and R?
questions.explanation: Lami's theorem: P/sinα = Q/sinβ = R/sinγ. With all angles = 120°: P/sin120° = Q/sin120° = R/sin120°. Therefore P = Q = R. Three equal forces at 120° to each other are always in equilibrium.

questions.question: A particle in equilibrium has two known forces: 6 N East and 8 N North. What is the third force needed for equilibrium?
questions.explanation: For equilibrium, third force must balance the resultant of the first two. Resultant of 6E and 8N = 10N at NE (bearing 045°). Balancing force = 10N at SW (bearing 045° + 180° = 225°).

questions.question: A body of weight W is suspended from a horizontal ceiling by two strings. One string makes 30° with the ceiling, the other makes 60°. Find the ratio of tensions T₁:T₂ (T₁ is in the 30° string).
questions.explanation: ΣFₓ=0: T₁cos60° = T₂cos30° (taking horizontal components toward the join point — wait: strings make angles with ceiling, so they make 60° and 30° with vertical). ΣFₓ=0: T₁sin30°=T₂sin60° (if these are angles with ceiling, component along ceiling). Actually: if angles are with ceiling: T₁cos(90°-30°)=T₁sin30° and T₂cos(90°-60°)=T₂sin60° are the horizontal components. ΣFₓ: T₁sin30°=T₂sin60° → T₁/2=T₂√3/2 → T₁=T₂√3 → T₁:T₂=√3:1.

lessons.title: Friction — Laws, Limiting Equilibrium, and Inclined Planes

content.body: Friction is the force that opposes relative motion (or tendency of motion) between two surfaces in contact. Without friction, we could not walk, cars could not brake, and buildings would slide off their foundations. Yet friction also causes wear, heat, and energy loss in machines. Understanding friction is essential for engineers designing roads, brakes, conveyor belts, and machinery. In Uganda, friction determines whether vehicles can safely climb steep hills like those in Kabale, whether boats remain stationary on sloping riverbanks, and whether landslides will occur on slopes after heavy rain.
Laws of Friction

items: Law 1: The friction force F opposes the direction of motion (or impending motion).
Law 2: The friction force is proportional to the normal reaction N: F ≤ μN, where μ (mu) is the coefficient of friction.
Law 3: At limiting equilibrium (on the verge of motion): F = μN. This is the maximum static friction.
Law 4: The kinetic friction (during motion) ≈ μₖN, where μₖ ≤ μₛ (kinetic ≤ static coefficient). At A-Level, we usually assume μₛ = μₖ = μ.
Law 5: Friction is independent of the area of contact (for smooth, rigid surfaces).
The coefficient of friction μ has no units. It depends only on the materials of the two surfaces. Typical values: rubber on dry road μ ≈ 0.7–0.8; steel on steel μ ≈ 0.1–0.3; ice on ice μ ≈ 0.03.
Angle of friction λ: tan λ = μ. When a body is in limiting equilibrium, the total reaction (resultant of N and F) makes angle λ with the normal.

content.body: Friction on a Horizontal Surface

content.title: Example 1: Minimum force to move a block on a horizontal surface
content.body:
A block of mass 8 kg rests on a rough horizontal surface (μ = 0.4). Find the minimum force needed to move the block. (g = 10 m/s²)

Forces: Weight W = 80 N down. Normal N = 80 N up (vertical equilibrium). Friction F = μN = 0.4 × 80 = 32 N (opposing motion).

For the block to just begin moving, applied force P must overcome limiting friction:
P = F = μN = 32 N (when force applied horizontally).

If force is applied at angle θ above horizontal:
Vertical equilibrium: N + P sinθ = W → N = W − P sinθ = 80 − P sinθ
Horizontal equilibrium (limiting): P cosθ = μN = μ(80 − P sinθ)
P cosθ + μP sinθ = 80μ = 32
P = 32/(cosθ + 0.4sinθ)

To minimise P: differentiate or use R-form. P is minimum when cosθ + 0.4sinθ is maximum.
Maximum of cosθ + 0.4sinθ = √(1+0.16) = √1.16 ≈ 1.077 at θ = arctan(0.4) ≈ 21.8°.
Minimum P = 32/1.077 ≈ 29.7 N at θ ≈ 21.8°.
Friction on an Inclined Plane
When a body rests on a rough inclined plane (angle α to horizontal), friction acts along the plane. If the body is in limiting equilibrium (about to slide), friction is at its maximum value μN.

content.title: Example 2: Body on a rough inclined plane
content.body:
A box of mass 10 kg rests on a rough inclined plane (α = 35°, μ = 0.5). (g = 10 m/s²)

a) Is the box in equilibrium?
b) Find the friction force acting.

Resolve perpendicular to plane: N = W cosα = 100 cos35° ≈ 81.9 N
Maximum friction available: F_max = μN = 0.5 × 81.9 = 41.0 N

Force trying to slide the box down: W sinα = 100 sin35° ≈ 57.4 N

Since 57.4 N > 41.0 N, friction cannot hold the box. The box will slide DOWN.

If the plane were only 20° steep:
W sin20° = 34.2 N. F_max = 0.5 × 100cos20° = 47.0 N.
34.2 < 47.0 → friction is sufficient → box remains in equilibrium.
Actual friction = 34.2 N (not at maximum — only what is needed).

content.title: Example 3: Body on rough inclined plane with applied force
content.body:
A body of mass 4 kg is on a rough inclined plane (α = 30°, μ = 0.3). A force P acts up the plane. Find P to:
a) Just prevent the body from sliding down
b) Just push the body up the plane
(g = 10 m/s²)

N = W cosα = 40 cos30° = 40 × (√3/2) = 20√3 N
Friction force magnitude: F = μN = 0.3 × 20√3 = 6√3 N
Component of weight down the plane: W sinα = 40 × 0.5 = 20 N

a) To prevent sliding DOWN: body tends to slide down, so friction acts UP the plane.
P + F − W sinα = 0 (equilibrium along plane)
P + 6√3 − 20 = 0
P = 20 − 6√3 ≈ 20 − 10.39 = 9.61 N

b) To push UP the plane: body tends to slide up, so friction acts DOWN the plane.
P − F − W sinα = 0 (equilibrium along plane, at limiting)
P = 20 + 6√3 ≈ 20 + 10.39 = 30.39 N

Interpretation: Any force P between 9.61 N and 30.39 N will keep the body stationary on the incline.

Road safety application: The maximum safe speed on a curved road depends on friction between tyres and road surface. A vehicle of mass m travelling at speed v around a curve of radius r needs centripetal force mv²/r. The maximum friction available is μmg. For safety: mv²/r ≤ μmg → v ≤ √(μgr). On a wet road (μ drops from 0.8 to 0.3), the safe speed drops dramatically — this is why speed limits are lower in wet conditions, and why Uganda's highways have warning signs at bends.

questions.question: A block of mass 5 kg rests on a horizontal surface with μ = 0.3. What is the friction force needed to keep it stationary when a horizontal force of 10 N is applied? (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: Normal N = mg = 50 N. Maximum friction = μN = 0.3×50 = 15 N. Applied force = 10 N < 15 N, so the block doesn't move. Static friction exactly balances the applied force = 10 N (not at maximum).

questions.question: A 6 kg box is on a rough horizontal surface (μ = 0.25). The minimum horizontal force to move it is: (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: N = mg = 60 N. Limiting friction = μN = 0.25 × 60 = 15 N. A horizontal force must exceed 15 N to start moving the box.

questions.question: A box slides down a rough inclined plane at constant velocity. Which statement is true?
options: There is no friction force acting
The applied force is greater than friction
The friction force equals the component of weight along the plane
The normal reaction equals the weight
questions.answer: The friction force equals the component of weight along the plane
questions.explanation: At constant velocity, acceleration = 0. By Newton's First Law, resultant force = 0. Along the plane: friction (up) = W sinα (down). Friction exactly balances the gravitational component along the plane.

questions.question: A body of mass 10 kg is on a rough incline (α = 30°). If the coefficient of friction is μ = 1/√3, find the friction force at limiting equilibrium. (g = 10 m/s²)
The angle of friction λ satisfies tan λ = μ = 0.6. What is the angle of friction to 1 decimal place?
questions.explanation: tan λ = 0.6 → λ = arctan(0.6) ≈ 30.96° ≈ 31.0°. So 30.9° is closest.

questions.question: A block of mass M rests on a rough inclined plane at angle α. It is in limiting equilibrium (about to slide). Which equation correctly relates α and μ?
options: μ = sin α/cos α = tan α
questions.explanation: At limiting equilibrium on an inclined plane with no external forces: friction F = W sinα (up plane), normal N = W cosα. F = μN → W sinα = μ W cosα → μ = tanα. The angle of limiting equilibrium equals the angle of friction: α = λ.

questions.question: A horizontal force of 30 N is needed to pull a 10 kg box at constant velocity along a horizontal floor. Find μ. (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: At constant velocity, friction = applied force = 30 N. N = mg = 100 N. μ = F/N = 30/100 = 0.30.

questions.question: A body on a rough inclined plane (μ = 0.4, α = 25°) is pushed UP the plane by a force P along the plane. Find the least force P. Mass = 5 kg, g = 10 m/s².
questions.explanation: N = 50cos25° ≈ 45.3 N. F = μN = 0.4×45.3 = 18.1 N (acting down plane when pushing up). P = Wsin25° + F = 50×0.4226 + 18.1 = 21.1 + 18.1 = 39.2 N.

lessons.title: Newton's Laws of Motion — Connected Particles and Pulley Systems

content.body: Newton's three laws of motion, published in 1687, remain the foundation of classical mechanics. They explain why objects move, how forces cause acceleration, and how objects interact. In this lesson, we apply Newton's Second Law (F = ma) systematically to solve problems involving single particles, connected particles (objects linked by strings), and pulley systems. These problems are central to understanding real engineering systems such as lifts, cranes, funiculars, conveyor belts, and towing vehicles — all of which appear in Uganda's growing infrastructure.
Newton's Laws — Summary

items: Newton's First Law: a body remains at rest or in uniform motion unless acted upon by a net (resultant) force. Inertia — the resistance of a body to changes in its state of motion — is explained by this law.
Newton's Second Law: the net force on a body equals its mass times its acceleration: F_net = ma. Direction of acceleration is the same as direction of net force.
Newton's Third Law: for every force exerted by body A on body B, body B exerts an equal and opposite force on body A. These action-reaction pairs act on DIFFERENT bodies.
In connected particle problems, the key insight is that all connected particles have the same acceleration (if the string is inextensible and the pulley is smooth).
Tensions in a string are equal throughout if the string is light (massless) and the pulley is smooth (frictionless).

content.body: The Standard Atwood Machine

An Atwood machine consists of two masses connected by a string over a smooth, fixed pulley. When released, the heavier mass descends and the lighter mass ascends. By applying Newton's Second Law to each mass separately and using the constraint that both have the same acceleration magnitude, we can find the acceleration and string tension.

content.title: Example 1: Basic Atwood machine
content.body:
Masses m₁ = 5 kg and m₂ = 3 kg are connected by a light inextensible string over a smooth pulley. Find the acceleration and tension. (g = 10 m/s²)

Let a = acceleration (m₁ goes down, m₂ goes up).

For m₁ (heavier, going down — take down as positive):
m₁g − T = m₁a
50 − T = 5a  ...(1)

For m₂ (lighter, going up — take up as positive):
T − m₂g = m₂a
T − 30 = 3a  ...(2)

Add (1) and (2):
50 − 30 = 8a
20 = 8a
a = 2.5 m/s²

From (2): T = 30 + 3(2.5) = 30 + 7.5 = 37.5 N

Verification from (1): 50 − 37.5 = 12.5 = 5 × 2.5 ✓

Formulas: a = (m₁−m₂)g/(m₁+m₂) = (5−3)×10/8 = 2.5 m/s²
T = 2m₁m₂g/(m₁+m₂) = 2×5×3×10/8 = 37.5 N

content.title: Example 2: Connected particles on a horizontal surface
content.body:
A 4 kg block A is connected to a 6 kg block B by a string. A force P = 50 N pulls B along a rough horizontal surface (μ = 0.2). The string between A and B is also on the surface. Find: (a) acceleration of the system, (b) tension in the string. (g = 10 m/s²)

Total friction on system = μ × total weight = 0.2 × (4+6) × 10 = 20 N

For the whole system (F_net = (m_A + m_B)a):
P − friction = (m_A + m_B)a
50 − 20 = 10a
a = 3 m/s²

For block A alone (pulled by tension T from string):
T − friction on A = m_A × a
T − 0.2 × 40 = 4 × 3
T − 8 = 12
T = 20 N

Verification for B: P − T − friction on B = m_B × a
50 − 20 − 0.2×60 = 6×3
50 − 20 − 12 = 18 = 6×3 ✓

content.title: Example 3: Particle on rough inclined plane connected to hanging mass
content.body:
A 3 kg mass A rests on a rough inclined plane (α=30°, μ=0.25), connected by a string over a smooth pulley at the top of the incline to a hanging mass B of 2 kg. Find the acceleration. (g=10 m/s²)

First check if the system moves:
Tendency: B pulls A up the plane.
Force pulling A up (from B): m_B g = 20 N
Force resisting motion up (gravity component + friction):
W_A sinα + μ W_A cosα = 30(0.5) + 0.25×30(√3/2) = 15 + 3.25√3 ≈ 15 + 5.63 = 20.63 N

Since 20 < 20.63, B cannot pull A up. Check if A slides down:
Force pulling A down (gravity component − friction from below):
W_A sinα − μ W_A cosα = 15 − 5.63 = 9.37 N
Weight of B resisting: 20 N. Since 9.37 < 20, A does not slide down either.
System is in equilibrium.

If μ = 0.1 instead:
Resistance up = 15 + 0.1×30(√3/2) = 15+2.6=17.6 N < 20 N → system moves!
a: For B: 20 − T = 2a ...(1). For A (up plane): T − 15 − 0.1×30cos30° = 3a
T − 15 − 2.6 = 3a → T = 17.6 + 3a ...(2)
Sub into (1): 20 − 17.6 − 3a = 2a → 2.4 = 5a → a = 0.48 m/s²

Lift engineering: A typical lift (elevator) in Kampala's high-rise buildings uses a counterweight connected by a cable over a pulley. The counterweight has mass close to the lift car + half-load, making the system nearly balanced. The motor only needs to supply force for the imbalance — greatly reducing energy consumption. Newton's second law and the Atwood machine equations directly describe the lift's motion during acceleration and deceleration.

questions.question: Two masses, 8 kg and 4 kg, are connected over a smooth pulley. Find the acceleration. (g = 10 m/s²)
For the Atwood machine in q1, find the tension in the string.
A 10 kg block is pulled across a smooth horizontal surface by a string. The string also connects to a 2 kg block hanging over the edge. Find the acceleration. (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: Net force = weight of hanging mass = 2×10 = 20 N. Total mass = 10+2 = 12 kg. a = 20/12 = 5/3 m/s².

questions.question: A 5 kg mass hangs from one end of a string. The other end is pulled upward with a force of 60 N. Find the acceleration. (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: F_net = 60 − 5×10 = 60 − 50 = 10 N upward. a = F/m = 10/5 = 2 m/s² upward.

questions.question: Two blocks A (3 kg) and B (5 kg) are connected by a light string. B is on a smooth horizontal table, A hangs over the edge. Find the tension in the string. (g = 10 m/s²)
In an Atwood machine, if m₁ = m₂ = m, what is the acceleration?
questions.explanation: a = (m₁−m₂)g/(m₁+m₂) = 0/(2m) = 0. Equal masses → no net force → no acceleration → system in equilibrium.

questions.question: A 4 kg block on a rough horizontal surface (μ = 0.3) is pulled by a 2 kg hanging mass via a string over a pulley. Find the acceleration. (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: Driving force = 2×10 = 20 N. Friction on 4 kg block = 0.3×40 = 12 N. F_net = 20−12 = 8 N. Total mass = 6 kg. a = 8/6 = 4/3 ≈ 1.33 m/s². Closest option: 1 m/s²? Let me check: a = (m_B×g − μ×m_A×g)/(m_A+m_B) = (20−12)/6 = 8/6 = 4/3 m/s² ≈ 1.33 m/s².

questions.question: A lift of mass 500 kg carries passengers of total mass 300 kg. The lift accelerates upward at 2 m/s². Find the tension in the lift cable. (g = 10 m/s²)

lessons.title: Applications of Newton's Laws — Acceleration, Equations of Motion, and Real-World Problems

content.body: This lesson consolidates Newton's laws and the equations of motion into a complete problem-solving framework. The equations of motion (also called SUVAT equations) apply to constant acceleration in a straight line. Combined with Newton's Second Law (F = ma), they allow us to find velocities, displacements, times, and forces for a wide range of real-world motion problems — from vehicles on Ugandan highways to falling bodies to objects sliding on surfaces. Mastery of these tools is essential for further study in physics and engineering.
Equations of Motion (SUVAT) — Uniform Acceleration

For uniform (constant) acceleration a, initial velocity u, final velocity v, displacement s, and time t:

v = u + at                    ... (1)
s = ut + ½at²                ... (2)
v² = u² + 2as                ... (3)
s = ½(u+v)t                  ... (4)
s = vt − ½at²                ... (5)

Sign convention: choose a positive direction (usually up or right). Forces and velocities in that direction are positive; opposing forces and velocities are negative.

G = 9.8 m/s² (or g = 10 m/s² for approximate calculations) acts DOWNWARD.
For a falling body: acceleration = +g (if taking down as positive).
For a thrown upward body: initial velocity is positive, acceleration = −g.

Free fall: any object in free fall (no air resistance) has acceleration g regardless of mass.

content.title: Example 1: Vehicle braking on a Ugandan highway
content.body:
A minibus travelling at 90 km/h brakes and comes to rest in 5 seconds. Find:
a) The deceleration
b) The braking force if the minibus has mass 2500 kg
c) The braking distance

Convert: u = 90 km/h = 25 m/s, v = 0, t = 5 s.

a) Using v = u + at:
0 = 25 + a(5) → a = −5 m/s² (deceleration of 5 m/s²)

b) F = ma = 2500 × 5 = 12,500 N = 12.5 kN (braking force)

c) Using s = ½(u+v)t = ½(25+0)(5) = 62.5 m
Alternatively: v² = u² + 2as → 0 = 625 − 10s → s = 62.5 m ✓

Implication: At 90 km/h on a wet road (where deceleration might only be 3 m/s²), the braking distance would be 625/(2×3) ≈ 104 m. Uganda Police set speed limits partly based on these calculations.

content.title: Example 2: Ball thrown vertically upward
content.body:
A ball is thrown vertically upward from the ground with initial velocity 20 m/s. (g = 10 m/s²)
Find: a) Maximum height, b) Time to reach maximum height, c) Time to return to ground

Taking upward as positive: u = 20 m/s, a = −10 m/s².

a) At maximum height, v = 0.
v² = u² + 2as: 0 = 400 − 20s → s = 20 m

b) v = u + at: 0 = 20 − 10t → t = 2 s

c) Return to ground: s = 0 (back at ground level).
s = ut + ½at²: 0 = 20t − 5t² → t(20 − 5t) = 0 → t = 0 (launch) or t = 4 s
Total time of flight = 4 s (2 s up + 2 s down ✓)

content.title: Example 3: Connected particles with friction — full analysis
content.body:
Mass A (6 kg) is on a rough horizontal table (μ = 0.3). It is connected by a string over a smooth pulley to mass B (4 kg) hanging freely. The system starts from rest. Find the velocity after B has fallen 2 m. (g = 10 m/s²)

Step 1 — Check motion: Driving force = m_B g = 40 N. Friction on A = μ × m_A g = 0.3×60 = 18 N. Net force = 40−18 = 22 N. System accelerates.

Step 2 — Find acceleration:
F_net = (m_A + m_B)a
22 = 10a → a = 2.2 m/s²

Step 3 — Find velocity after s = 2 m:
v² = u² + 2as = 0 + 2(2.2)(2) = 8.8
v = √8.8 ≈ 2.97 m/s

Step 4 — Find tension (for B):
m_B g − T = m_B a
40 − T = 4(2.2) = 8.8
T = 31.2 N

content.title: Example 4: Particle on inclined plane — finding time
content.body:
A 2 kg block starts from rest at the top of a smooth inclined plane (length 5 m, α = 30°). Find the time to reach the bottom and the speed at the bottom. (g = 10 m/s²)

a = g sinα = 10 × sin30° = 10 × 0.5 = 5 m/s² (down the plane)

Using s = ut + ½at²:
5 = 0 + ½(5)t² = 2.5t²
t² = 2 → t = √2 ≈ 1.41 s

Using v² = u² + 2as:
v² = 0 + 2(5)(5) = 50
v = √50 = 5√2 ≈ 7.07 m/s

Alternatively using energy: v = √(2gh) = √(2×10×5sin30°) = √50 ✓

Athlete biomechanics: When a Ugandan sprinter accelerates from the starting blocks at the national athletics stadium in Kampala, Newton's second law governs their motion. The force from leg muscles provides acceleration. Friction between spikes and track provides the reaction force. The SUVAT equations can predict the time to reach maximum speed and the distance covered. Sports scientists use these calculations to optimise training programmes and technique.

questions.question: A car starts from rest and reaches 30 m/s in 10 seconds. Find the acceleration and driving force if the car has mass 1200 kg.
A stone is dropped from a height of 45 m. How long does it take to hit the ground? (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: s = ½gt²: 45 = ½(10)t² = 5t². t² = 9. t = 3 s.

questions.question: A train decelerates uniformly from 72 km/h to rest. If the deceleration is 2 m/s², find the braking distance.
A ball is thrown upward at 15 m/s. What is the maximum height reached? (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: v=0 at max height. v²=u²+2as: 0=225−20s → s=225/20=11.25 m.

questions.question: A 5 kg block is pushed along a rough surface (μ = 0.4) by a 30 N horizontal force. Find the acceleration. (g = 10 m/s²)
A 3 kg particle slides down a smooth inclined plane (α = 45°). Find the velocity after sliding 4 m from rest. (g = 10 m/s²)
A particle is projected vertically upward. It reaches maximum height after 3 s. Find the initial velocity and the total time of flight. (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: At max height, v = 0. v = u−gt: 0 = u−10(3) → u = 30 m/s. By symmetry, time to fall back = 3 s. Total flight time = 6 s.

questions.question: A 1000 kg car accelerates from 10 m/s to 25 m/s over 100 m on a level road. Find the net driving force.

key_concepts: Force is a vector: has magnitude (N) and direction. Forces are resolved into horizontal (F cosθ) and vertical (F sinθ) components.
Resultant by component method: ΣFₓ = sum of horizontal components; ΣF_y = sum of vertical; R = √((ΣFₓ)²+(ΣF_y)²); direction: tan θ = ΣF_y/ΣFₓ.
Equilibrium conditions: ΣFₓ = 0 AND ΣF_y = 0. The triangle of forces theorem applies to three forces in equilibrium.
Lami's theorem: for three concurrent coplanar forces P, Q, R in equilibrium: P/sinα = Q/sinβ = R/sinγ (α, β, γ are angles BETWEEN the forces).
Friction laws: F ≤ μN. At limiting equilibrium: F = μN. Friction opposes motion (or impending motion). Angle of friction λ: tanλ = μ.
Newton's Second Law: F_net = ma. Applied to each particle separately in connected-particle problems.
Atwood machine: a = (m₁−m₂)g/(m₁+m₂); T = 2m₁m₂g/(m₁+m₂). Same acceleration for both masses.
SUVAT equations: v=u+at; s=ut+½at²; v²=u²+2as; s=½(u+v)t. Apply with consistent sign convention.
For inclined plane: acceleration = g sinα (smooth); resolve parallel and perpendicular to plane for friction problems.
common_errors: Resolving forces incorrectly — confusing sinθ and cosθ. Remember: component along the direction of θ uses cosine.
Forgetting to include friction in both directions when a body can potentially slide up or down an incline.
Using the wrong sign convention in SUVAT — being inconsistent with positive direction across the problem.
Treating tension as the same as weight in Atwood machine — they are different. T = m(g−a) for the heavier mass.
Applying Lami's theorem with angles to axes instead of angles between forces.
Neglecting to check whether a system actually moves before applying kinetic friction equations.
Forgetting to add friction forces when pulling a block with a string (friction on EACH block individually).

questions.question: A force of 15 N acts at 60° to a 20 N horizontal force. Find the resultant using the cosine rule.
Four forces act on a particle: 10N East, 6N North, 4N West, 8N South. Find the resultant.
questions.explanation: ΣFₓ=10−4=6N East. ΣF_y=6−8=−2N (South). R=√(36+4)=√40=2√10≈6.32N. tan θ=2/6=1/3 → θ=arctan(1/3)≈18.4° East of South. Bearing = 180°−18.4° = 161.6°? From North clockwise: South is 180°, then 18.4° toward East → bearing = 180°−18.4° = 161.6°.

questions.question: A 4 kg mass rests on a smooth plane inclined at 30°, held by a force parallel to the plane. Find the tension and normal reaction. (g = 10 m/s²)
A block of mass 8 kg sits on a rough horizontal surface (μ = 0.35). What is the maximum force that can act horizontally without causing motion? (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: N = mg = 80 N. Maximum static friction = μN = 0.35×80 = 28 N. Any horizontal force up to 28 N can be applied without motion.

questions.question: Masses of 6 kg and 2 kg hang from the ends of a string over a smooth pulley. The 6 kg mass descends 3 m from rest. Find its velocity. (g = 10 m/s²)
A particle is in equilibrium under three forces: 8 N at 0°, 6 N at 90°, and F at angle θ. Find F and θ.
options: F = 10 N, θ = 53.13° below the negative x-axis
questions.explanation: ΣFₓ=0: 8+Fcosθ=0 → Fcosθ=−8. ΣF_y=0: 6+Fsinθ=0 → Fsinθ=−6. F=√(64+36)=10N. θ=arctan(−6/−8) in QIII: base=arctan(6/8)=36.87°. θ=180°+36.87°=216.87°. Hmm: the angle is in QIII (both components negative). 180°+36.87°=216.87°. But answer says 233.13°? tan(angle)=6/8=0.75, arctan=36.87°. In QIII: 180°+36.87°=216.87°.

questions.question: A body slides at constant velocity down a rough inclined plane. If α = 20°, find μ.
questions.explanation: At constant velocity, net force = 0. Along plane: Wsinα = F = μN = μWcosα. μ = tanα = tan20° ≈ 0.364.

questions.question: A car of mass 1500 kg accelerates at 2 m/s² on a level road against a resistance of 500 N. Find the driving force.
questions.explanation: F_net = ma = 1500×2 = 3000 N. F_driving = F_net + resistance = 3000+500 = 3500 N.

questions.question: A string connecting two particles (3 kg and 5 kg) on a smooth surface breaks when the tension exceeds 20 N. A force F pulls the 5 kg particle. Find the maximum F before the string breaks. (g = 10 m/s²)
A ball is thrown horizontally at 10 m/s from a cliff 20 m high. Find the time to hit the ground and the horizontal distance. (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: Vertical: 20 = ½×10×t² → t²=4 → t=2 s. Horizontal: s = 10×2 = 20 m.

questions.question: A 10 kg mass is on a rough incline (α=30°, μ=0.4). Find whether the mass slides and the friction force. (g=10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: Wsinα=100×0.5=50N (trying to slide down). N=Wcosα=100×(√3/2)=50√3≈86.6N. Max friction=μN=0.4×86.6=34.64N. Since 50N>34.64N, the mass SLIDES. Actual friction=34.64N (at maximum). So: slides; friction=34.6N.

questions.question: Two masses of 7 kg and 3 kg are connected by a string over a smooth pulley. The 7 kg mass is on a rough horizontal surface (μ = 0.2); the 3 kg mass hangs freely. Find the acceleration. (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: Driving force=3×10=30N. Friction on 7kg=0.2×7×10=14N. F_net=30−14=16N. Total mass=10kg. a=16/10=1.6 m/s².

questions.question: A 2 kg particle falls from rest through air resistance of 4 N. Find the acceleration. (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: F_net = mg − R = 2×10 − 4 = 20−4 = 16 N downward. a = F/m = 16/2 = 8 m/s².

questions.question: A vehicle of mass 2000 kg travels at constant speed up an incline (sinα = 0.05) against a resistance of 400 N. Find the driving force. (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: At constant speed, F_net = 0. Driving force = Weight component up incline + resistance = mg sinα + R = 2000×10×0.05 + 400 = 1000+400 = 1400 N.

questions.question: In an Atwood machine, masses 12 kg and 8 kg are released. After 2 s, the heavier mass has descended a distance of:
